landing.title: Season 2026 Engineering Notebook
landing.content: Welcome to SWERVO 26256's engineering notebook. This year, our focus is on robust autonomous paths and reliable hardware. We began our journey by prototyping our [Drivetrain Code] to ensure smooth field navigation. At the same time, our hardware subteam started work on an [Active Intake System] to reliably acquire elements. We invite you to read through our iterations and learn about our engineering process.

sections.title: Software
sections.content: Welcome to the Software directory. Here you will find all of our code iterations, control algorithms, and odometry mathematics.

subsections.title: Drivetrain Code
subsections.content: This folder contains all iterations of our Mecanum drive subsystem code, from early prototyping to our final PID implementations.

subsections.title: Iteration 1
subsections.author: Aaron
subsections.content: Today we wrote the initial mecanum drive code using the [REV Control Hub] and tested encoder feedback. The primary goal was to ensure smooth strafing and consistent odometry tracking. We utilized a custom PID controller for rotational stability.

subsections.title: Sensors & Logic
subsections.content: Documentation regarding sensor integration, logic flows, and hardware-software communication protocols.

subsections.title: Encoder Calibration
subsections.author: Aaron
subsections.content: We implemented dead-wheel odometry to accurately track robot position regardless of wheel slip. Calibrating the track width took several attempts, but we finally achieved a precision of +/- 1cm over a full field translation.

sections.title: Hardware
sections.content: Welcome to the Hardware directory. Here you will find our physical iterations, CAD prototypes, and mechanical reasoning.

subsections.title: Active Intake System
subsections.content: Intake geometry research, material selection, and speed calculations.

subsections.title: Prototyping Phase
subsections.author: Sarah
subsections.content: Initial tests with compliant wheels showed promise, but we encountered slipping when acquiring elements from off-angles. We adjusted the geometry to a 30-degree approach angle.

subsections.title: Electronics
subsections.content: Custom circuit boards, cable management, and power distribution diagrams.

subsections.title: REV Control Hub Mounting
subsections.author: Sarah
subsections.content: We securely mounted the REV Control Hub centrally to ensure the IMU is perfectly aligned with the robot's center of rotation. Wiring was routed cleanly to avoid entanglement with moving mechanisms.
